_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2
title: Two ways to reason
deck: Almost every problem we solve uses one of two reasoning moves. Naming them is what turns guessing into thinking on purpose.
objectives: Apply deductive and inductive reasoning to solve problems.

content.text: Imagine standing in the coffee line on a Tuesday morning. Every time we have ordered a large iced coffee here, the cashier has rung us up for $5.50. So today we reach for the same five-fifty before we even glance at the menu board.

That move is one kind of reasoning. Suppose, instead, we had memorized the menu the night before, and we knew the rule outright: every large drink costs $5.50. Now reaching for the same coins is not a guess at all. It is an application of a stated rule. Both paths arrived at the same answer, but they gave us very different kinds of certainty. This section gives those two paths their proper names and shows when each one can be trusted.
1.2.1  Two directions of reasoning

Both moves aim at a conclusion, but they walk in opposite directions. Inductive reasoning starts with specific cases and generalizes a pattern from them. Deductive reasoning starts with a general rule and applies it to a specific case. The difference matters because of certainty: a deductive conclusion is guaranteed whenever the rule is true, while an inductive conclusion is only probable.
Specific to general is induction. General to specific is deduction.

Figure 1.2 captures the two directions side by side. On the left, a stated rule ("all A are B") flows downward into a specific case ("this X is an A, so this X is a B"). On the right, a stack of specific observations ("these A's are all B's") flows upward into a proposed pattern.

content.caption: Figure 1.2. Deductive reasoning flows top-down from a rule to a case. Inductive reasoning flows bottom-up from cases to a pattern.

content.text: Definition 1.2. Inductive reasoning generalizes a pattern from a collection of specific cases. Deductive reasoning applies a general rule to a specific case.

Margin noteBoth terms are Latin in origin. Inducere means "to lead into," the move that leads us into a general rule. Deducere means "to lead down from," the move that leads us down from a rule to a specific case.
1.2.2  Inductive reasoning: cases lead to a pattern

Inductive reasoning is the move we make whenever we say “this restaurant is usually crowded on Fridays,” or “I have never seen a black swan.” We are looking at a stack of past cases and extending the pattern forward. The conclusion is strong when the cases are many and consistent, but it is never beyond doubt: a single contrary case is enough to knock it over.

An inductive conclusion is called a conjecture, a careful word for “a pattern-based guess that may still turn out to be wrong.” The conjecture “all swans are white” was held for centuries by Europeans, then dropped immediately the day a Dutch explorer met a black swan in Australia in 1697. One observation undid every previous example.
Common mistakeTreating an inductive conclusion as if it were guaranteed. A pattern is evidence, not a proof. The day someone shows us a counterexample, every previous case stops mattering.
1.2.3  Deductive reasoning: rules lead to certainty
Deductive reasoning turns a stated rule into a stated answer. If we accept the rule and the case truly falls under it, the conclusion is locked in. It cannot be wrong unless the rule itself is wrong.

A small chain illustrates the move. Suppose the MAT-144 syllabus states that any student who misses more than three classes will be administratively withdrawn. Marcus has missed four classes. So, by the syllabus, Marcus will be administratively withdrawn. The conclusion follows from the rule and the case with no room for doubt, provided the rule is enforced.
1.2.4  How the two work together

In real problem solving, we almost always use induction first to spot a pattern, then deduction to apply the rule we now believe in. Example 1.2 below traces that sequence, starting from a dorm-Wi-Fi mystery and ending with a confident prediction.

Example 1.2. The case of the disappearing Wi-Fi.

Your dorm Wi-Fi keeps cutting out, and we want to know why, and when it will happen next. We will use induction to find the pattern, then deduction to predict the next outage.

Solution.

Step 1. Collect observations. We write down the time of every drop this week:

DayTime of drop
Monday7:02 PM
Tuesday7:00 PM
Wednesday7:05 PM

Step 2. Generalize the pattern (induction). Three nights, same time. We propose a conjecture: the Wi-Fi drops around 7 PM. It is not certain yet, but it is a strong starting point.

Step 3. Find the underlying rule. We ask the RA, who explains that the dorm router is overwhelmed when too many devices stream at once, and at 7 PM roughly two hundred students start their evening Netflix episode. Now we have a real rule: the router overloads when too many devices stream simultaneously.

Step 4. Predict (deduction). It is Thursday at 6:55 PM. By the rule, the router will overload as soon as the 7 PM streaming begins. We do not have to wait and see. At 7:01 PM the Wi-Fi dies, exactly as the rule predicted.

Induction got us close: we noticed a pattern and made a useful conjecture. Deduction got us certain: once we knew the underlying rule, the next outage was guaranteed. Most real problem solving moves through these two steps, in this order.

In the wildDetective novels, medical diagnoses, and scientific research all run on the same two-step rhythm. An investigator sees a pattern in the evidence (induction), proposes a rule for what is happening, and then deduces what must happen next if the rule holds. When the prediction matches reality, the rule earns more trust. When it fails, the rule has to be revised.
1.2.5  Counterexamples kill "all" statements

A counterexample is a single case that breaks a universal claim. Because deductive reasoning rests on the truth of the rule, finding one case where the rule fails is enough to throw out the entire conclusion. Counterexamples are powerful precisely because we only need one of them.

For instance, the claim “all MAT-144 students attend Tuesday classes” is destroyed the moment we find Jordan, a MAT-144 student who only attends on Mondays and Wednesdays. No amount of additional supporting cases can rescue the “all” statement once a single contradicting case exists.
Exercises

1. Sarah notices that the last five times she ate spicy food before bed, she had trouble sleeping. She concludes that spicy food at night keeps her awake. Is her reasoning inductive or deductive?

    Show answer

    Answer. Inductive. Sarah gathered specific observations (five nights of poor sleep after spicy food) and generalized them into a pattern. Her conclusion is probable, not guaranteed; a counterexample (eating spicy food and sleeping fine) would weaken it.

  2. The MAT-144 syllabus states that any student who misses more than three classes will be administratively withdrawn. Marcus has missed four classes. His advisor concludes that Marcus will be withdrawn. Is the advisor reasoning inductively or deductively?

    Show answer

    Answer. Deductive. The advisor started with a general rule (the syllabus) and applied it to a specific case. As long as the rule is enforced, the conclusion is guaranteed.

  3. Examine the sequence 3, 7, 11, 15, …. Each value is four more than the one before it, so the next value should be 19. Identify the kind of reasoning used.

    Show answer

    Answer. Inductive. We looked at specific examples and generalized a pattern. The prediction is almost certainly correct, but in principle the next value could follow a different rule.

  4. Which statement best describes the difference between deductive and inductive reasoning?

    1. Deductive reasoning is always wrong; inductive is always right.
    2. Deductive conclusions are guaranteed when the rule is true; inductive conclusions are probable but not certain.
    3. Deductive reasoning uses numbers; inductive uses words.
    4. There is no real difference between the two.
    Show answer

    Answer. B. The defining difference is certainty: a deductive conclusion follows necessarily from its rule, while an inductive conclusion remains a strong guess.

  5. A store posts: “Spend more than $50 and you get free shipping.” You spend $73. Using deductive reasoning, what can you conclude?

    Show answer

    Answer. The shipping is free, guaranteed by the rule. The rule covers any total above $50, and $73 is above $50, so the conclusion follows with certainty.

  6. Someone claims: “All MAT-144 students attend Tuesday classes.” Which observation would serve as a counterexample?

    1. Most MAT-144 students attend Tuesday classes.
    2. Maria attends MAT-144 every Tuesday.
    3. Tuesday classes are popular at GCU.
    4. Jordan is enrolled in MAT-144 but only attends Mondays and Wednesdays.
    Show answer

    Answer. D. A counterexample is a single case that breaks a universal claim. Jordan is a MAT-144 student who does not attend Tuesday, and one such case is enough to falsify an “all” statement.
More worked examples

content.title: Deductive reasoning — apply a known rule
content.problem: All squares have four equal sides. The shape ABCD is a square. What can you conclude about side AB?
content.solution:
**Apply the rule.** The general statement says every square has four equal sides. ABCD is given as a square, so it inherits the property.

Therefore **side AB has the same length as sides BC, CD, and DA**.

This is *deductive* reasoning: from a general rule, we derive a guaranteed specific conclusion. If the rule is correct and the premise is correct, the conclusion is certain.

content.title: Inductive reasoning — spot a pattern, then test it
content.problem: A pattern shows the outputs **3, 7, 11, 15, …** when the inputs are **1, 2, 3, 4, …**. What is the rule, and what is the 10th term?
content.solution:
**Look for a constant difference.** Each output is **4 more** than the one before. That suggests a linear pattern of the form y = 4x + b.

Plug in x = 1 to find b: 3 = 4(1) + b, so b = −1. The rule appears to be y = 4x − 1.

Predict the **10th term**: y = 4(10) − 1 = **39**.

*The trap to notice.* Inductive reasoning produces a *conjecture*, not a guaranteed truth. The first four data points fit y = 4x − 1, but they would also fit a more complicated formula. The pattern is plausible, not certain, until we are told the rule or shown more data.

content.title: Summary
content.content: **Deductive** reasoning starts from a general rule and produces a guaranteed conclusion. **Inductive** reasoning starts from specific cases and proposes a general rule that fits — plausible, not certain. Both are essential in math: induction generates conjectures; deduction verifies them.

content.text: Looking ahead

Section 1.3 sharpens one of the most common inductive moves: the everyday guess that “this should cost about fifty bucks” or “that meeting should run about an hour.” That move is called estimation, and a handful of small techniques make it remarkably reliable, and remarkably good at catching errors a calculator would never notice.
1.2
